title: Brass and copper cleaning paste
subtitle: A salt, flour, and vinegar paste that removes tarnish and verdigris from brass and copper
excerpt: Copper tarnishes to a deep brown and can develop green verdigris in damp conditions. Both are copper oxide compounds dissolved by the acetic acid in white vinegar. Salt and flour form a mildly abrasive binder that holds the paste against the surface while the acid does its work.
sourceNotes: Brass and copper cleaning chemistry from standard household chemistry references.

recipe.yieldDescription: Small batch (enough for one cleaning session)
recipe.batchNotes: Mix only what is needed per session; the paste does not store well.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Mix fresh for each use. Leftover paste can be stored in a sealed container for 24 hours at most before discarding.

glossaryTerms.definition: The green or blue-green patina that forms on copper and brass surfaces, consisting mainly of copper carbonate and copper acetate. It is a surface compound and is removed by acids such as vinegar.
The active acid in white vinegar (typically 5% acetic acid). It reacts with copper oxide and copper carbonate compounds to dissolve tarnish and verdigris from brass and copper surfaces.
Plain flour acts as a neutral binder in cleaning pastes, holding the abrasive and acid components in contact with the surface being cleaned. It rinses away cleanly with water.

content.text: Brass and copper tarnish forms through oxidation;

in white vinegar, which dissolves the oxide layer. Salt provides a mild abrasive to assist the cleaning action, and

holds the paste together on the surface during the dwell time. This method does not harm lacquered brass; however, check whether your brassware is lacquered before using, as the acid will strip lacquer. Mix fresh for each use.

items.prepNote: 30% of dry ingredients; acts as binder
Added in small amounts to form a stiff paste; approximately 2 to 3 tablespoons

content.text: Combine the salt and flour in a small bowl. Add white vinegar a teaspoon at a time, stirring after each addition, until the mixture forms a thick paste that holds its shape. Apply the paste to the tarnished brass or copper surface and leave for 10 minutes. For heavy tarnish or verdigris, leave for up to 30 minutes.

Scrub gently with a soft cloth or old toothbrush, then rinse thoroughly under running water. Dry immediately with a clean cloth. Do not leave wet surfaces; copper and brass will re-tarnish quickly if left damp.
Mix fresh for each cleaning session. Leftover paste will dry out and the flour can ferment. Discard any unused paste after 24 hours.
